answer:
Runtime vs Compile-time at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

Runtime vs Compile-time is the fundamental TypeScript design axis: compile-time checks (types, interfaces, generics) are erased at runtime, meaning TypeScript cannot validate data crossing system boundaries (API responses, user input, deserialized payloads). At 1M+ users, a scalable strategy bridges this gap with schema validation libraries (Zod, io-ts) that generate both compile-time types and runtime validators from a single source, ensuring type safety extends from database to UI without duplication or drift.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system consumed Prisma-generated types at compile time, but API endpoints received raw JSON from Angular forms. A junior developer trusted TypeScript types alone — the startDate field was typed as Date but arrived as a string at runtime. This caused silent data corruption: PostgreSQL stored ISO strings instead of proper timestamps, breaking date arithmetic in leave balance calculations. Manoj introduced Zod schemas as the single source of truth: z.coerce.date() for date fields, with types derived via z.infer. This unified compile-time safety and runtime validation in one place, eliminating an entire class of type-boundary bugs.

📊 Compile-time to Runtime Safety Bridge

💻 Code Example — Unified Compile-time + Runtime Validation

import { z } from 'zod';

// Single source of truth: Zod schema
const LeaveRequestSchema = z.object({
  employeeId: z.string().regex(/^EMP-\d{6}$/, 'Invalid employee ID format'),
  type: z.enum(['annual', 'sick', 'maternity', 'paternity', 'compensatory', 'unpaid']),
  startDate: z.coerce.date(),  // Converts string → Date at runtime
  endDate: z.coerce.date(),
  reason: z.string().min(10).max(500),
}).refine(
  data => data.endDate > data.startDate,
  { message: 'End date must be after start date', path: ['endDate'] }
);

// Compile-time type derived from schema
type LeaveRequest = z.infer;
// = { employeeId: string, type: 'annual' | ..., startDate: Date, endDate: Date, reason: string }

// Fastify route — validates at runtime boundary
app.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  // req.body is unknown at this point
  const result = LeaveRequestSchema.safeParse(req.body);

  if (!result.success) {
    return reply.code(400).send({
      errors: result.error.issues.map(i => ({
        field: i.path.join('.'),
        message: i.message
      }))
    });
  }

  // result.data is fully typed LeaveRequest with actual Date objects
  const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
    data: result.data  // TypeScript verified + runtime validated
  });

  return reply.code(201).send(leave);
});

// Environment variables — runtime validated at startup
const EnvSchema = z.object({
  DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
  JWT_SECRET: z.string().min(32),
  PORT: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(65535).default(3000),
  NODE_ENV: z.enum(['development', 'production', 'test']),
});

export const env = EnvSchema.parse(process.env);
// Crashes at startup if env vars invalid — fail fast

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Leave balance calculations were off by 1-2 days for some employees. Manoj traced the bug to PostgreSQL: startDate was stored as '2025-03-15T00:00:00.000Z' (string) instead of a proper timestamp. Prisma's DateTime type expected a Date object but received a string at runtime — TypeScript didn't catch this because the type annotation said Date. After adding z.coerce.date(), the Zod schema converted the ISO string to a proper Date object before it reached Prisma. Manoj also added a CI migration script to fix existing records: UPDATE leave_requests SET start_date = start_date::timestamp WHERE pg_typeof(start_date) = 'text'. He made it a team rule: "If data crosses a boundary, it goes through Zod."

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Trusting TypeScript at API boundariesRuntime data doesn't match compile-time typesZod/io-ts validation at every external boundary
Duplicate type definitions (TS interface + Zod schema)Types drift out of syncDerive TS types from Zod: z.infer<typeof Schema>
as assertions bypassing validationCasts skip runtime checks; bugs reintroducedESLint rule banning as except in test files; prefer satisfies
Performance cost of runtime validationZod parse adds latency on hot pathsValidate once at boundary; trust types internally; benchmark critical paths

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Boundary coverage audit: Every API endpoint, env var, and external data source must have a Zod schema
  • Type derivation check: CI script verifying no manual interface duplicates Zod schema shapes
  • Coercion tests: Unit tests for each Zod schema with string inputs → verify proper type conversion
  • Startup validation: App crashes immediately if env vars fail Zod validation (fail fast)
  • ESLint enforcement: Ban as type assertions in production code; allow only satisfies

Runtime vs Compile-time — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Runtime vs Compile-time performance bottlenecks emerge from two axes: (1) compile-time — complex types slowing tsc and IDE responsiveness, and (2) runtime — validation overhead from schema libraries adding latency to hot paths. Instrumentation involves measuring tsc diagnostics for compile-time performance, and runtime profiling of validation costs to find the optimal balance between safety and speed.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub's bulk CSV upload validated 500 contacts per batch through a Zod schema. Initial profiling showed Zod validation consuming 180ms per batch — 36% of total processing time. Manoj instrumented the validation with performance.now() and identified nested .refine() calls checking cross-field constraints (phone format varies by country) as the bottleneck. He restructured: simple field validation via Zod (fast, 20ms), cross-field validation via a custom function after Zod parsing (batched, 40ms). Total validation dropped from 180ms to 60ms. For the compile-time axis, he measured tsc --extendedDiagnostics and found Zod's type inference for the contacts schema added 2s to compilation — acceptable given the safety benefit.

📊 Validation Performance Optimization

💻 Code Example — Optimized Validation Pipeline

import { z } from 'zod';

// Step 1: Zod for structural validation (fast, 20ms for 500 contacts)
const ContactSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  email: z.string().email(),
  phone: z.string().min(7).max(20),
  country: z.string().length(2),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).max(10).default([]),
});

type Contact = z.infer;

// Step 2: Custom cross-field validation (batched, 40ms for 500)
function validateCrossField(contacts: Contact[]): ValidationResult[] {
  // Batch validation — check duplicates in one pass
  const emailSet = new Set();
  const results: ValidationResult[] = [];

  for (const contact of contacts) {
    const errors: string[] = [];

    // Cross-field: phone format depends on country
    if (contact.country === 'IN' && !contact.phone.match(/^\+91\d{10}$/)) {
      errors.push('Indian phone must be +91XXXXXXXXXX');
    }

    // Batch duplicate detection
    if (emailSet.has(contact.email)) {
      errors.push(`Duplicate email: ${contact.email}`);
    }
    emailSet.add(contact.email);

    results.push({ contact, errors });
  }
  return results;
}

// Optimized bulk validation pipeline
async function validateBulkUpload(rawContacts: unknown[]) {
  const t0 = performance.now();

  // Step 1: Structural validation via Zod
  const zodResults = rawContacts.map(c => ContactSchema.safeParse(c));
  const valid = zodResults
    .filter((r): r is z.SafeParseSuccess => r.success)
    .map(r => r.data);
  const zodTime = performance.now() - t0;

  // Step 2: Cross-field validation
  const t1 = performance.now();
  const crossFieldResults = validateCrossField(valid);
  const crossTime = performance.now() - t1;

  console.log(`Validation: Zod ${zodTime.toFixed(0)}ms, cross-field ${crossTime.toFixed(0)}ms`);

  return {
    valid: crossFieldResults.filter(r => r.errors.length === 0),
    invalid: crossFieldResults.filter(r => r.errors.length > 0),
    zodErrors: zodResults.filter(r => !r.success)
  };
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During load testing ContactsHub's bulk upload with 500 contacts, the endpoint took 500ms instead of the expected 200ms. Manoj added performance.now() instrumentation at each pipeline stage and found Zod's .refine() calls were the bottleneck: each contact's cross-field validation created new closures and ran regex matches inside the Zod pipeline. Moving cross-field logic to a separate batched function reduced overhead because: (1) duplicate detection used a single Set across all contacts instead of per-contact DB lookups, and (2) phone validation regexes were compiled once, not per-contact. He also profiled compile-time impact: Zod's type inference for the schema added 2s to tsc, which was acceptable. The team established a rule: Zod for structure, custom validators for business rules.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Zod in hot loops without batchingValidation overhead scales linearly with itemsParse structural fields only; batch cross-field checks
Complex Zod schemas slowing compilationIDE lag, slow CI buildsMonitor tsc --extendedDiagnostics; split schemas if > 5s impact
Skipping runtime validation for "trusted" internal dataBugs when data shape changes unexpectedlyValidate at all boundaries; skip only for same-process function calls
Zod .transform() side effectsTransform errors hard to trace; data mutation surprisesKeep transforms pure; separate transformation from validation

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Validation benchmark: Vitest bench for Zod schemas with 500-item arrays; < 100ms budget
  • Compile-time monitoring: tsc --extendedDiagnostics in CI; alert if Zod schemas add > 5s
  • Coverage report: Audit all API boundaries for Zod schema presence; 100% coverage target
  • Instrumented pipeline: Every validation stage logged with timing for production monitoring
  • Regression test: Bulk upload 500 contacts → assert total time < 300ms including validation

Type-safe API Clients — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Type-safe API client performance bottlenecks arise from: code generation overhead (regenerating client types on every schema change), runtime wrapper layers (generic fetch wrappers adding overhead), response validation cost (Zod parsing every API response), and bundle size impact (generated types inflating the client bundle). Instrumentation measures generation time, request/response latency attribution, validation overhead, and bundle contribution to optimize the type-safety-to-performance trade-off.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard consumed 15 Express.js endpoints. Initially, API types were manually maintained — 3 endpoints had drifted types causing silent data loss (pipeline stage field renamed on backend but not frontend). Manoj implemented a type-safe API client using openapi-typescript to generate types from the Express Swagger spec, plus a thin createApiClient<T> wrapper providing typed methods. The generated types added 45 KB to the bundle (acceptable) but the openapi-typescript generation step took 8s in CI. He optimized by caching the generated file and only regenerating on schema changes, reducing CI time by 8s for most builds.

📊 Type-safe API Client Architecture

💻 Code Example — Type-safe API Client with Performance Controls

import type { paths } from './generated/api-types';
import createClient from 'openapi-fetch';

// Type-safe client from OpenAPI spec
const api = createClient({
  baseUrl: 'https://api.crm.example.com',
});

// Fully typed request and response
async function getLeads(page: number, filters: LeadFilters) {
  const { data, error } = await api.GET('/api/leads', {
    params: {
      query: { page, pageSize: 20, ...filters }
      // TypeScript enforces: only valid query params
    }
  });

  if (error) {
    // error is typed based on OpenAPI error responses
    throw new ApiError(error.message, error.code);
  }

  // data is typed as PaginatedList
  return data;
}

// Dev-only response validation (skip in production for performance)
const apiWithValidation = createClient({
  baseUrl: process.env.API_URL,
  ...(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' && {
    middleware: [{
      async onResponse(res) {
        const body = await res.clone().json();
        // Validate response matches expected type
        const schema = getSchemaForEndpoint(res.url);
        if (schema) {
          const result = schema.safeParse(body);
          if (!result.success) {
            console.warn('API response type mismatch:', result.error);
          }
        }
        return res;
      }
    }]
  })
});

// Cache-aware type generation in CI
// scripts/generate-api-types.sh
// #!/bin/bash
// SPEC_HASH=$(md5sum swagger.json | cut -d' ' -f1)
// if [ -f .api-types-hash ] && [ "$(cat .api-types-hash)" = "$SPEC_HASH" ]; then
//   echo "API types up to date, skipping generation"
//   exit 0
// fi
// npx openapi-typescript swagger.json -o src/generated/api-types.d.ts
// echo "$SPEC_HASH" > .api-types-hash

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

The CRM Dashboard's sales pipeline widget showed "undefined" for lead priority after a backend sprint renamed the field from priority to urgency. The manual TypeScript interface still had priority: string. After introducing openapi-typescript, Manoj regenerated the types from the updated Swagger spec — TypeScript immediately flagged 4 components accessing lead.priority (now lead.urgency). The rename was completed in 15 minutes. He also added a CI step: if swagger.json changed, regenerate types and fail the build if any TypeScript errors result — catching API contract changes before they reach production.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Generated types not regenerated after API changesTypes drift; silent data bugsCI hook: regenerate on swagger.json change; fail on type errors
Response validation in productionAdds latency to every API callValidate in dev/staging only; trust types in production
Large generated type filesIDE slowdown, bundle bloatUse .d.ts files (not in bundle); split by API domain
Codegen tool version mismatchDifferent developers generate different typesPin codegen tool version; commit generated types

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Schema change detection: CI hash check on swagger.json; regenerate types on change
  • Type compilation gate: tsc --noEmit after regeneration; zero errors required
  • Dev-mode validation: Response schema validation in development catches drift early
  • Generation time budget: openapi-typescript < 10s; cache when spec unchanged
  • Bundle impact audit: Verify generated types use .d.ts extension (not included in bundle)

Type-safe API Clients — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

Type-safe API clients provide compile-time guarantees that client code matches server API contracts. Approaches include: (1) code-generated clients from OpenAPI/Swagger specs, (2) tRPC for end-to-end type inference without code generation, (3) GraphQL codegen for typed queries, and (4) shared type packages. An interviewer-grade understanding covers trade-offs between these approaches, when code generation vs. runtime inference is appropriate, handling API versioning with typed clients, and the DX vs. maintenance burden of each approach.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system's Angular frontend consumed 12 Fastify REST endpoints. With the Angular HttpClient, response types were manually typed — leading to 3 incidents where the frontend expected fields the backend no longer returned (after schema migrations). Manoj evaluated tRPC (rejected — Angular doesn't have first-class tRPC support), GraphQL codegen (rejected — overkill for 12 REST endpoints), and openapi-typescript (selected — generates types from Fastify's auto-generated Swagger spec). The generated client caught all future schema mismatches at compile time and served as living API documentation for the frontend team.

📊 Type-safe Client Approach Comparison

💻 Code Example — OpenAPI-typed Fastify + Angular Client

// Backend: Fastify with auto-generated Swagger spec
import fastifySwagger from '@fastify/swagger';

await app.register(fastifySwagger, {
  openapi: {
    info: { title: 'HR Leave API', version: '1.0.0' },
  },
});

// Route with JSON schema (auto-generates OpenAPI spec)
app.post('/api/leaves', {
  schema: {
    body: {
      type: 'object',
      required: ['employeeId', 'type', 'startDate', 'endDate'],
      properties: {
        employeeId: { type: 'string', pattern: '^EMP-\\d{6}$' },
        type: { type: 'string', enum: ['annual', 'sick', 'maternity'] },
        startDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date-time' },
        endDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date-time' },
      }
    },
    response: {
      201: {
        type: 'object',
        properties: {
          id: { type: 'string' },
          status: { type: 'string', enum: ['pending', 'approved', 'rejected'] },
        }
      }
    }
  }
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const leave = await createLeave(req.body);
  return reply.code(201).send(leave);
});

// Frontend: Angular service with generated types
import type { paths } from '../generated/hr-api';

type CreateLeaveBody = paths['/api/leaves']['post']['requestBody']['content']['application/json'];
type CreateLeaveResponse = paths['/api/leaves']['post']['responses']['201']['content']['application/json'];

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class LeaveApiService {
  constructor(private http: HttpClient) {}

  createLeave(body: CreateLeaveBody): Observable {
    return this.http.post('/api/leaves', body);
    // TypeScript enforces request body shape AND response type
  }
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After a Prisma migration renamed leaveType to type in the database and Fastify schema, the Angular frontend still sent { leaveType: 'annual' }. The request succeeded (Fastify stripped unknown fields) but the leave was created with type: null. After setting up openapi-typescript generation in CI, the Angular build immediately failed: Property 'leaveType' does not exist on type CreateLeaveBody. Did you mean 'type'?. The fix took 2 minutes. Manoj made the Swagger spec generation a CI step that runs before the frontend build, ensuring type mismatches are caught before deployment.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Swagger spec not auto-generatedManual spec drifts from implementationUse Fastify's schema-to-swagger; auto-generate spec from code
Ignoring generated type errorsTypes generated but not enforced in buildCI pipeline: regenerate types → tsc --noEmit → fail on errors
Over-reliance on generated types without runtime validationAPI returns unexpected data at runtimeZod validation at client boundary for critical endpoints
Generated client coupling frontend to backend versioningAPI version changes break all clients simultaneouslyVersion-stamped generated types; support N-1 version during migration

✅ Validation Strategy

  • CI type generation: Regenerate types from Swagger spec on every backend change; frontend build fails on mismatch
  • Contract test: Pact-style test verifying Angular client expectations match Fastify responses
  • Spec accuracy: Integration test comparing actual API responses against Swagger spec
  • Version compatibility: Test matrix running current frontend against N-1 API version
  • Living documentation: Swagger UI served from Fastify for frontend developers to browse

TS Config — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

TypeScript Config (tsconfig.json) performance bottlenecks arise from: misconfigured compiler options (e.g., missing skipLibCheck checking all node_modules types), overly broad file inclusion patterns, disabled incremental compilation, and strict mode options that increase type-checking complexity. Instrumentation involves tsc --extendedDiagnostics for timing, --generateTrace for type-computation profiling, and build tool integration metrics to optimize the compile-time experience.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system's Angular 18 project had a monolithic tsconfig.json that included test files, story files, and configuration scripts in the main compilation. With 200+ source files plus 150 test files, tsc took 55s. Manoj restructured to a project references architecture: tsconfig.app.json (source only, 25s), tsconfig.spec.json (tests, references app), and tsconfig.e2e.json (Playwright, standalone). Combined with incremental: true and skipLibCheck: true, the day-to-day incremental build dropped to 4s. CI full builds went from 55s to 28s via project references enabling parallel type-checking.

📊 TSConfig Project References Architecture

💻 Code Example — Optimized TSConfig Structure

// tsconfig.json — shared base
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,           // Skip type-checking node_modules
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,         // Enables single-file transpilation
    "incremental": true,             // Cache previous compilation
    "tsBuildInfoFile": ".tsbuildinfo"
  }
}

// tsconfig.app.json — source code only
{
  "extends": "./tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "composite": true,               // Enable project references
    "declaration": true,
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src"
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*.ts"],
  "exclude": ["src/**/*.spec.ts", "src/**/*.test.ts"]
}

// tsconfig.spec.json — tests reference app types
{
  "extends": "./tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "composite": true,
    "types": ["vitest/globals"]
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*.spec.ts", "src/**/*.test.ts"],
  "references": [
    { "path": "./tsconfig.app.json" }
  ]
}

// Build command leveraging project references
// tsc --build tsconfig.app.json tsconfig.spec.json
// Parallel type-checking: app and spec can be checked in parallel

// CI performance monitoring script
// tsc --extendedDiagnostics --noEmit 2>&1 | tee tsc-report.txt
// grep -E 'Check time|Program time|Bind time|Parse time' tsc-report.txt

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

CI builds were taking 55s for TypeScript compilation, slowing the team's feedback loop. Manoj ran tsc --extendedDiagnostics and found: Parse time: 3s, Bind time: 5s, Check time: 42s, Emit time: 5s. The 42s check time was dominated by type-checking 150 test files that were included in the main compilation. Restructuring to project references separated test checking from source checking. Adding skipLibCheck: true eliminated 8s spent checking @types/* packages. Enabling incremental meant subsequent builds only re-checked changed files — dropping the inner-loop rebuild to 4s. He added a CI step logging tsc --extendedDiagnostics output to a metrics dashboard, setting alerts if check time exceeded 35s.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Missing skipLibCheckTypeScript checks all .d.ts in node_modules (8-15s overhead)Always enable skipLibCheck: true; rely on package tests for their own types
Overly broad include patternsConfig files, scripts, stories included in compilationExplicit include with exclude for non-source files
Missing incrementalFull recompilation on every changeEnable incremental; commit .tsbuildinfo to cache in CI
strict: false to "speed up" compilationMarginal speed gain (< 5%) but massive type safety lossAlways use strict: true; optimize via project references instead

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Compile time budget: tsc --extendedDiagnostics in CI; alert if check time > 35s
  • Project reference integrity: tsc --build --dry to verify reference graph is valid
  • Incremental cache hit rate: Log .tsbuildinfo cache hits; target > 80% on incremental builds
  • IDE responsiveness: Developer survey on go-to-definition and autocomplete latency
  • Strict mode audit: CI check verifying strict: true in all tsconfig files

TS Config — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

📘 Definition

TypeScript Configuration (tsconfig.json) controls the compiler's behavior across type-checking strictness, module resolution, output format, and project structure. An interviewer-grade understanding covers: the trade-off matrix of strict options (strictNullChecks, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes), module resolution strategies (node vs bundler vs node16), project references for monorepo scaling, path aliases and their bundler integration, and the relationship between tsconfig and build tools (Angular CLI, Vite, webpack).

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub evolved through 3 frontend generations (Vanilla JS → Angular → React 18 with Vite). Each migration required careful tsconfig tuning. The React/Vite setup needed: moduleResolution: 'bundler' (Vite-compatible), jsx: 'react-jsx' (automatic JSX transform), paths aliases matching Vite's resolve.alias, and isolatedModules: true (required by Vite's esbuild transpilation). Manoj documented every tsconfig decision in a decision log, preventing future developers from accidentally changing options that would break the Vite build (e.g., removing isolatedModules would allow const enum usage that esbuild can't handle).

📊 TSConfig Decision Matrix

💻 Code Example — Production TSConfig Patterns

// ContactsHub: React 18 + Vite tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    // Module system — must match Vite's expectations
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",  // Vite-compatible resolution
    "jsx": "react-jsx",             // Automatic JSX transform (no React import needed)
    "isolatedModules": true,         // Required: esbuild transpiles per-file

    // Strict type safety
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, // array[i] is T | undefined
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true,
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true,

    // Path aliases (must match vite.config.ts resolve.alias)
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["./src/*"],
      "@components/*": ["./src/components/*"],
      "@hooks/*": ["./src/hooks/*"],
      "@api/*": ["./src/api/*"]
    },

    // Performance
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "incremental": true,
    "tsBuildInfoFile": ".tsbuildinfo"
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*.ts", "src/**/*.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist", "**/*.spec.ts"]
}

// vite.config.ts — must mirror tsconfig paths
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import path from 'path';

export default defineConfig({
  resolve: {
    alias: {
      '@': path.resolve(__dirname, './src'),
      '@components': path.resolve(__dirname, './src/components'),
      '@hooks': path.resolve(__dirname, './src/hooks'),
      '@api': path.resolve(__dirname, './src/api'),
    }
  }
});

// Fastify backend tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "NodeNext",          // Node.js native ESM
    "moduleResolution": "nodenext", // Requires .js extensions in imports
    "declaration": true,            // Generate .d.ts for shared types
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "strict": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "incremental": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*.ts"],
  "exclude": ["src/**/*.test.ts"]
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After migrating ContactsHub from Angular to React/Vite, imports using @/components/ContactList worked in VS Code (resolved by tsconfig paths) but failed at runtime (Vite didn't know about them). The root cause: tsconfig paths and Vite resolve.alias were not synchronized. Manoj added a CI check: a script that parses both tsconfig.json paths and vite.config.ts aliases, asserting they match. He also discovered that a developer used const enum (which requires whole-program compilation) — but Vite's esbuild transpiles per-file (isolatedModules). The build silently converted the const enum to a regular enum, changing runtime behavior. He added an ESLint rule to ban const enum when isolatedModules is enabled.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
TSConfig paths not synced with bundler aliasesIDE resolves but runtime failsCI script comparing tsconfig paths and vite/webpack aliases
const enum with isolatedModulesSilently changes to regular enum; different runtime behaviorESLint rule: no-const-enum when isolatedModules: true
Wrong moduleResolution for build toolImport resolution differs between TypeScript and bundlerbundler for Vite, node for webpack, nodenext for Node.js
Disabling strict options "temporarily"Technical debt accumulates; bugs reintroducedCI check: strict: true required; no per-file @ts-ignore without JIRA ticket

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Path alias sync: CI script comparing tsconfig paths with bundler aliases; fail on mismatch
  • Strict mode enforcement: CI check verifying all tsconfig files have strict: true
  • isolatedModules check: ESLint rules enforcing compatible patterns (no const enum, no namespace merging)
  • Build parity test: tsc --noEmit + vite build both must succeed; if one fails and other doesn't → config drift
  • Decision log: Every tsconfig option documented with rationale and project context
